title: My Degree Got Me to the Starting Line. Everything After That I Had to Figure Out Myself.
excerpt: Nobody tells you about the gap.
body:
Nobody tells you about the gap.



The gap between graduating and actually knowing what you are doing. Between having a degree and having the skills the market actually pays for. Between the classroom and the real world.



I graduated with a B.S.B.A. in Accounting and Finance from Kutztown University with a 3.32 GPA. I studied hard. I showed up. I did everything the traditional path told me to do.



And then I walked into the real world and realized the degree had gotten me to the starting line.



That was it.



Everything after the starting line I had to figure out on my own.



The classroom taught me the vocabulary. GAAP. Debits and credits. Financial statement analysis. Capital budgeting. The theoretical framework that lets you walk into a professional conversation and not embarrass yourself.



That part was valuable. I am not going to pretend otherwise.



But the vocabulary is not the skill. Knowing what a balance sheet is and being able to build one that a client actually needs are two completely different things.



QuickBooks is the software that runs the books of most small businesses in America. It was barely touched in my coursework. I learned it on my own. Studied for the ProAdvisor certification myself. Earned Gold status at Level 1, Level 2, and Payroll through my own time and effort outside of any classroom.



The FINRA SIE and Series 30 licenses I hold. Self studied. Passed on my own.



The Enrolled Agent exam I am working toward. Self directed.



The LLC I founded and operate. Nobody taught me how to do that in a lecture hall.



The website I built from scratch with schema markup, geo targeting, and local SEO. Not in the curriculum.



The corporate strategy project I completed for Reynolds American. The talent pipeline tool I am building for United Talent Agency. The full stack web application I built for a nonprofit newsroom.



None of that was in the syllabus.



I am not saying college was worthless. The degree opened doors that might have stayed closed without it. Some opportunities require it as a checkbox and I had the checkbox.



But I think we owe people especially young people deciding whether to spend four years and $80,000 on a degree a more honest conversation about what that degree actually delivers.



It delivers the starting line.



It does not deliver the race.



The race is figured out through licenses you earn yourself. Skills you build on your own time. Projects you take on before anyone asks you to. Credentials that prove you can actually do the thing not just that you sat in a room where someone explained it.



If I could go back I would still get the degree. But I would do it faster. WGU offers competency based accounting programs where you move at the speed you actually learn. Flat rate per term. Finish faster pay less. No sitting in classes waiting for everyone else to catch up.



What I would not do is assume the degree was the destination.



It was always just the door.



What you do after you walk through it is entirely on you.



And nobody in the classroom tells you that.



What did you have to figure out on your own after graduation that you expected the degree to teach you?



Matthew Coleman is the owner of MCC Solutions LLC, a licensed financial services firm in Cranford NJ. He holds active FINRA SIE and Series 30 licenses, QuickBooks ProAdvisor Gold certification, and is pursuing his Enrolled Agent designation and Series 66.
